title: Financiers
subtitle: Small French almond cakes made with browned butter and egg whites
excerpt: Financiers are small, dense almond cakes made from browned butter, ground almonds, egg whites, icing sugar, and a minimal amount of flour. The batter is made quickly once the butter is browned and rested: the browned butter is the primary flavour, and the egg whites make the texture simultaneously moist and slightly springy. Baked in rectangular or oval moulds at high heat, they develop a caramelised crust.
sourceNotes: Financiers are a small French pâtisserie cake, named either for the financial district of Paris near which they were sold or for their gold-bar shape. They are associated with the pâtissier Lasne near the Paris Bourse (stock exchange) in the 19th century, though the basic formula of browned butter, ground almonds, and egg whites appears in earlier French pastry literature. The defining technique is the beurre noisette: the butter is cooked until the milk solids turn golden and the aroma shifts to nuts and caramel.

content.text: . Brown the butter carefully: stop it as soon as the milk solids turn golden and the nutty smell arrives. Pour it immediately into a cool bowl to stop the cooking. Let it rest until warm but not hot before mixing with the other ingredients. The batter rests for an hour so the brown butter can infuse the almonds; it can also rest overnight in the fridge.

Cook the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat, swirling occasionally, until the milk solids turn golden and the aroma becomes nutty (about 5 minutes). Pour into a large bowl and leave to cool until warm but not hot.

Stir the ground almonds, icing sugar, flour, and salt into the brown butter. Add the egg whites and honey and stir until smooth. The batter should be pourable. Cover and rest at room temperature for 1 hour (or refrigerate overnight).

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Butter the cavities of a 12-hole mini muffin tin or financier mould generously. Fill each cavity three-quarters full with batter. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until deep golden at the edges and just set in the centre. Leave in the tin for 3 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Serve warm or at room temperature.
